name: Uri Geller
description: Uri Geller is an Israeli illusionist, magician, television personality, and self-proclaimed psychic. He is known for his trademark spoon-bending feats, which he attributes to psychokinesis or the power of the mind. Born in Tel Aviv, Geller rose to fame in the 1970s, captivating audiences worldwide with demonstrations of seemingly impossible abilities, including starting stopped watches, telepathy, and influencing dice rolls. Proponents believe Geller possesses genuine psychic powers, citing his repeated successes under various controlled and uncontrolled conditions as evidence. Geller himself has attributed his abilities to extraterrestrial contact or a connection to a higher consciousness. However, mainstream science and the skeptical community overwhelmingly consider Geller's performances to be elaborate illusions and stage magic. Critics, including prominent magicians such as James Randi, have replicated Geller's feats using conventional magic techniques, arguing that Geller relies on misdirection, suggestion, and subtle trickery. Furthermore, controlled scientific experiments designed to test Geller's abilities have generally yielded negative results, failing to provide statistically significant evidence of genuine psychic phenomena. Despite the lack of scientific validation, Geller remains a popular figure, continuing to perform and promote his claims of psychic abilities.
subtitle: Israeli psychic and spoon-bending illusionist

claims.evidence: Visual demonstrations on television and in live performances; anecdotal accounts of spontaneous spoon bending.
claims.criticism: Demonstrations replicated by magicians using sleight of hand and misdirection. No scientifically verifiable evidence of psychokinesis.

claims.evidence: Claims of reading minds and transmitting thoughts during performances; anecdotal evidence from audience members.
claims.criticism: Techniques such as cold reading, hot reading, and suggestion can explain perceived telepathic feats. Lack of controlled experiments demonstrating telepathy.

claims.claim: Starting stopped watches through mental influence
claims.evidence: Demonstrations of starting broken watches on television and in live performances.
claims.criticism: Stopped watches can sometimes restart due to mechanical issues being temporarily resolved by handling or temperature changes. No evidence of mental influence.

claims.claim: Influence over dice rolls and other random events.
claims.evidence: Claims to be able to influence outcomes of dice rolls and other random events.
claims.criticism: Outcomes of dice rolls are statistically independent. Claims of influence are attributed to chance or selective reporting of favorable outcomes.

keyFigures.name: James Randi
keyFigures.role: Magician and skeptic, debunker of Geller's claims

keyFigures.name: Martin Gardner
keyFigures.role: Science writer and skeptic, critic of Geller's claims

keyFigures.name: Andrija Puharich
keyFigures.role: Physician and parapsychologist, early proponent of Geller

mythologyConnections: Ancient Oracles (claimed parallels to accessing divine knowledge)
Shamanism (claimed parallels in accessing altered states of consciousness)

timeline.event: Geller rises to international fame with his psychic performances.
BBC broadcast featuring Geller's spoon-bending sparks controversy and debate.
Geller transitions into business and design, licensing his name for various products.
Geller continues to appear on television and promote his claims, often in reality show formats.

sources.author: Andrija Puharich
sources.work: Uri: A Journal of the Mystery of Uri Geller
sources.title: Andrija Puharich
